title: Assistant-Guided Decoding
short_definition: A speed trick where a small fast model drafts the next several words and a big model only checks them, accepting good guesses and rejecting bad ones.
full_explanation:
Generating one token at a time on a 70B model is slow because every step does a full forward pass. Assistant-guided decoding (the umbrella name for techniques like speculative decoding and EAGLE) speeds this up by enlisting a small 'draft' model. The small model proposes a chunk of tokens, then the big model verifies the entire chunk in a single forward pass. Tokens the big model would have produced anyway get accepted for free; the first mismatch resets the draft.

The math works because the verification pass costs roughly the same wall-clock time as generating one token, so if even half the draft tokens are accepted you net 2x speedup. Production systems push this further: Medusa adds multiple draft heads to the main model, and EAGLE trains a draft network specifically for the target model's distribution. Llama.cpp, vLLM, and TensorRT-LLM all ship implementations.

The tradeoff is memory: you now hold two models in VRAM (or extra parameters for the draft heads). When the draft model disagrees often — different domains, unusual prompts — the speedup collapses and you've paid the memory cost for nothing.
why_it_matters: Inference cost dominates LLM economics, and this technique cuts latency 2-4x without changing model quality at all — verified outputs are bit-identical to standard sampling. For anyone serving an API, that's the difference between $0.30 per million tokens and $0.10. Open-source serving stacks adopted this aggressively in 2024-2025 because it's the rare optimization that's both free quality-wise and dramatic speed-wise. If you're running a self-hosted Llama setup and haven't enabled it, you're leaving 50% throughput on the table.
how_to_think_about_it: Picture a senior engineer reviewing a junior engineer's pull requests. The junior writes drafts quickly; the senior scans them in batches, accepting clean code and rewriting the rest. Both end up with the same output the senior would have written alone, but the senior's expensive time is spent on review (parallel) instead of writing line-by-line (serial). The whole game is keeping junior-acceptance rate high — pair them well or the senior just rewrites everything anyway.
see_it_in_practice: vLLM's speculative decoding flag is one line: `--speculative-model meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B --num-speculative-tokens 5`. Pair a 1B draft with a 70B target and watch tokens-per-second jump 2-3x on the same hardware. EAGLE has its own repo at github.com/SafeAILab/EAGLE with pre-trained draft heads for popular open models.

related_terms_explained.connection: The original 2022 technique that assistant-guided decoding generalizes from.
A faster variant where the draft network is trained jointly with the target model.
Speculative methods need careful KV cache management to roll back rejected tokens.
The metric that improves most — tokens/second often jumps 2-4x with no quality loss.

title: Beam Search
short_definition: A decoding method that keeps the top N most-likely sentences alive in parallel and picks the best complete one at the end, instead of committing word-by-word.
full_explanation:
Standard greedy decoding picks the single highest-probability token at every step. The problem: a high-probability first word can lock you into a low-probability sentence overall. Beam search hedges by keeping multiple candidate sequences (the 'beams') alive simultaneously, each with a running probability score. At every step, every beam expands by every possible next token, then the algorithm prunes back to the top N total candidates by score.

With beam width 4, you explore 4 sequences in parallel and pick the most probable one when they all hit an end-of-sequence token. The cost is exactly N times more compute than greedy decoding. For tasks with a clear right answer — translation, summarization, code generation — beam search consistently beats greedy on metrics like BLEU and ROUGE. It dominated machine translation for years (Google Translate used it) and remains standard for sequence-to-sequence models like T5 and BART.

The major drawback: beam search produces boring text. All beams converge on the highest-probability path, which for open-ended generation means generic, repetitive output. This is why ChatGPT-style chatbots use temperature sampling instead — beam search makes them sound like a thesaurus.
why_it_matters: Beam search defines the 'right answer' decoding strategy across most NLP tasks that aren't open-ended chat. If you're building a translator, summarizer, or structured-output extractor, you almost certainly want beam search and not sampling — the quality bump on benchmark metrics is consistent and significant. Open-source frameworks like HuggingFace Transformers ship beam search as a single argument (`num_beams=4`). The reason most LLM tutorials skip it is that chat applications happened to be the dominant use case for the past three years, but for the rest of NLP it's still the default.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine planning a road trip. Greedy says: take the most direct road at every intersection. You might hit traffic. Beam search says: keep 4 plausible routes open at every fork, score each by total estimated time, prune to the best 4, repeat. At the end you commit to the route with the lowest total time. More work, but you avoid the 'locally optimal, globally terrible' trap. Width 1 is just greedy. Width 100 is overkill — past width 5-10 returns vanish.
see_it_in_practice: In HuggingFace: `model.generate(input_ids, num_beams=4, early_stopping=True)`. For translation, try widths 4-8. For code generation, sometimes width 2 with a length penalty does well. Diagnostic tip: if all your beams produce identical output, your prompt is too constrained — sampling won't help either, and you should look at the prompt, not the decoder.

related_terms_explained.connection: Beam search with width 1 — the simplest baseline beam search improves on.
A variant that adds a penalty to make the beams explore different sequences.
Sampling-based alternative — beam search is deterministic; temperature adds randomness.
Beam search optimizes for low perplexity, which sometimes correlates with quality and sometimes doesn't.

code_example:
# Beam search in HuggingFace Transformers
out = model.generate(
    input_ids,
    num_beams=4,
    early_stopping=True,
    no_repeat_ngram_size=3,
    length_penalty=1.0,
)

title: Constrained Decoding
short_definition: Forcing an LLM's output to match a specific format — like valid JSON, a regex pattern, or a finite list of options — by zeroing out token probabilities that would break the format.
full_explanation:
LLMs occasionally produce malformed JSON, hallucinate enum values, or break a strict schema. Retrying with parsing fallbacks works but wastes tokens. Constrained decoding solves this at the sampling step: at each token position, the decoder computes which next tokens are legal given the constraint (a JSON schema, regex, or grammar), then masks every illegal token's logit to negative infinity before sampling. The model can only emit something valid.

Mechanically, the constraint is compiled to a finite-state machine. After each generated token, the FSM advances to its next state, which exposes a new set of allowed transitions. For JSON, this means: after `{` only a quote is allowed; after a string key, only `:` is allowed; and so on. The model still chooses among legal options based on its learned probabilities — you're not forcing content, just blocking malformed structure.

Production implementations include Outlines (Python, FSM-based), Guidance (Microsoft, template-driven), and llama.cpp's grammar support. OpenAI ships a JSON schema mode; Anthropic ships tool-use mode. Both use server-side constrained decoding under the hood. The cost is that you can break the model's natural distribution badly with overly restrictive grammars — the model trained on natural text doesn't always know how to satisfy your schema gracefully.
why_it_matters: If you're shipping LLM features in production, broken JSON is a Tuesday-afternoon incident waiting to happen. Constrained decoding turns 'parse-or-retry' into 'always parses, full stop.' That single guarantee removes huge categories of error handling and makes function-calling, structured extraction, and multi-step agents reliable enough for paying customers. Open-source has made this universally accessible — Outlines works with any HuggingFace model in five lines. Closed-source providers charge for it implicitly via tool-use APIs, so for self-hosters this is one of the bigger 'free wins' available.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of it like guardrails on a bowling lane. The bowler (the model) still picks how hard to throw and where to aim, but the gutters are blocked off. The ball can still hit gutters of strategy — bad data, weird answers — but it physically cannot leave the lane. Constrained decoding is structural safety, not semantic safety. It guarantees the JSON parses; it does not guarantee the JSON contains correct values.
see_it_in_practice: With Outlines: `import outlines; gen = outlines.generate.json(model, MyPydanticSchema); result = gen(prompt)`. Returns a validated Python object every time. Combined with a small fast model like Qwen-2.5-1.5B you get production-grade structured extraction at <50ms per call.

related_terms_explained.connection: Constrained decoding is the most reliable way to enforce structured output.
Function-calling APIs like OpenAI's tools mode use constrained decoding under the hood.
The leading open-source library implementing this technique for HuggingFace models.
A simpler manual version — logit bias masks individual tokens, constrained decoding masks dynamically per position.
A specific implementation using context-free grammars compiled to FSMs.

code_example:
# Outlines: guarantee valid JSON matching a Pydantic schema
from pydantic import BaseModel
import outlines

class User(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int

generator = outlines.generate.json(model, User)
user = generator('Extract: John is 32')
# user is a validated User object — never a parse error

title: Contrastive Decoding
short_definition: A decoding strategy that makes a big LLM produce more interesting output by subtracting a small LLM's predictions, surfacing tokens the big model uniquely prefers.
full_explanation:
Greedy decoding from a strong model often picks the most generic continuation — the kind any model would produce. Contrastive decoding (Li et al., 2022) reframes the goal: at each step, sample the token that maximizes the difference between the strong model's probability and a weak 'amateur' model's probability. The big model's unique knowledge gets amplified; shared boilerplate gets suppressed.

Mechanically, you run two forward passes per token — one through your target model, one through a smaller model (often a 1B or 7B variant of the same family). The decoder picks tokens that the big model rates highly AND the small model rates poorly. A length-penalty tunable controls aggression. The result: less repetition, more substantive content, and measurably better performance on open-ended generation benchmarks like creative writing and reasoning.

Follow-up techniques like DoLa (Decoding by Layer-wise Contrasting) extend this idea to layers within a single model — contrast the final layer's predictions against an early layer's, on the theory that early layers encode 'common' knowledge and late layers encode the model's true expertise. DoLa improved factuality on TruthfulQA by 12-18% with no fine-tuning.
why_it_matters: This category of techniques has had a quiet but real impact on serving open-source models. They don't change the model — no retraining needed — but they squeeze out 5-15% on quality benchmarks for free. For practitioners running open-weight models locally and looking for more out of fixed weights, contrastive methods are usually the second thing to try after sampling parameter tuning. Closed APIs don't expose this knob, so it's an open-source-specific lever. Worth understanding when you're trying to push a self-hosted setup past the obvious quality ceiling.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine asking two friends what to cook for dinner. The novice always says 'pasta.' The chef says 'pasta' too because it's safe — but if you ask 'what would you cook that the novice wouldn't?' the chef gets specific: cacio e pepe with hand-rolled tonnarelli. Contrastive decoding is the second question, applied to every token. The answer is always more interesting because it surfaces what's unique to the expert.
see_it_in_practice: HuggingFace's generate() supports it via `penalty_alpha=0.6, top_k=4`. A simpler signal: if your model's outputs feel formulaic and you've already tuned temperature, try contrastive search before reaching for fine-tuning.

related_terms_explained.connection: Contrastive decoding is the 'anti-greedy' alternative that explicitly avoids generic continuations.
A layer-wise contrast variant that uses a single model's internals instead of two models.
Contrastive decoding addresses a different problem (genericness) than temperature (randomness).
Both use two models, but contrastive picks based on probability gaps; speculative just verifies.

code_example:
# Contrastive search in HuggingFace
out = model.generate(
    input_ids,
    penalty_alpha=0.6,
    top_k=4,
    max_new_tokens=200,
)

title: Deterministic Decoding (Seeded Generation)
short_definition: Configuring an LLM to produce the exact same output every time you give it the same input — usually for testing, debugging, or reproducible research.
full_explanation:
By default, LLMs generate slightly different responses to the same prompt because sampling pulls from a probability distribution. This is fine for chat but terrible for tests, evals, and research. Deterministic decoding eliminates the randomness through one of two routes: set temperature to 0 (effectively making the model pick the highest-probability token every time), or set a fixed random seed so the sampling RNG produces the same draws.

Temperature 0 is the simpler approach but isn't fully deterministic on GPUs — floating-point summation order varies across batch sizes and CUDA versions, so you can still see one-in-a-thousand divergences. Seeded decoding with the model in eval mode is closer to true determinism, but only on the same hardware and library version. Research papers usually note both: 'We set temperature=0 and seed=42 for all experiments.'

Most serving frameworks expose both knobs: vLLM accepts `seed=42`, OpenAI's API has a `seed` parameter (though their docs note it's best-effort, not guaranteed), and llama.cpp supports `--seed N`. For evals, snapshot tests, and CI pipelines that compare model outputs, this is non-negotiable.
why_it_matters: If you're writing tests against an LLM and they pass sometimes but not others, you don't have a flaky test — you have an unsealed source of randomness. Deterministic decoding is the foundation for any LLM-based CI pipeline, regression eval, or A/B comparison where you need to attribute changes in output to changes in your code rather than chance. For reproducible research it's a baseline expectation; for production debugging it's how you reliably reproduce a bad output a customer reported.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of a coin flip versus reading a book. A coin flip gives you a different result each time — that's normal sampling. Reading the book on page 47 always gives you the same words — that's deterministic decoding. The model's probability distribution is the book; sampling decides whether you flip or you read. For features where users want freshness, flip. For tests where you want reproducibility, read.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI Python: `client.chat.completions.create(model='gpt-4o', messages=msgs, temperature=0, seed=42)`. Then assert the response matches a snapshot. If it diverges, you have a real change to investigate — not noise.

related_terms_explained.connection: Setting temperature=0 is the simplest path to (mostly) deterministic output.
Greedy decoding is the deterministic baseline — it always picks the top token.
Deterministic decoding is one of several requirements for fully reproducible LLM experiments.
Snapshot tests against LLM output only work with deterministic decoding.

code_example:
# Deterministic generation across runs
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=[{'role':'user','content':'Tell me a joke'}],
    temperature=0,
    seed=42,
)

title: Diverse Beam Search
short_definition: A beam search variant that splits beams into groups and penalizes groups for producing the same words, forcing the model to generate genuinely different candidates.
full_explanation:
Standard beam search has a known failure: all N beams converge on slight variations of the same sentence. If you ask for the top 5 captions of an image, you get five paraphrases of one caption. Diverse beam search (Vijayakumar et al., 2016) splits the beams into G groups and runs them sequentially, applying a diversity penalty to each new group based on the tokens already generated by previous groups. The penalty discourages repetition across groups while still rewarding high-likelihood sequences within each group.

With 6 beams split into 3 groups of 2, you get 3 distinct candidates instead of 6 near-duplicates. The diversity strength is a hyperparameter (typically 0.3-1.0). Higher values produce more varied outputs at the cost of average quality — extreme values can make non-top-group beams emit nonsense. The technique shines in tasks where you want options: caption generation, brainstorming, paraphrase generation, or n-best translation candidates.

HuggingFace exposes it as `diversity_penalty` and `num_beam_groups` in `model.generate()`. Mostly used in academic settings and for product features that surface multiple suggestions (think: code completion that shows 3 distinct options).
why_it_matters: Useful in a narrow but real set of cases: anywhere you're presenting users with multiple options and 'top 5 versions of the same sentence' would feel broken. Image captioning, code suggestion lists, content generation tools that show alternatives, and academic n-best decoding evaluations all benefit. For chat applications using temperature sampling already, this isn't relevant — sampling provides diversity for free. But for deterministic structured tasks that need both quality and variety, this is the standard approach.
how_to_think_about_it: If standard beam search is asking five candidates the same interview question, you'll get five very similar answers because top performers all know the textbook response. Diverse beam search is interviewing them in groups and forbidding each new group from repeating prior answers. Group 1 gives the textbook answer. Group 2 has to find a different angle. Group 3 has to find another. You end up with five genuinely different perspectives instead of five copies of one.
see_it_in_practice: HuggingFace: `model.generate(input_ids, num_beams=6, num_beam_groups=3, diversity_penalty=0.7, num_return_sequences=6)` returns 6 candidates with meaningful variation. Compare that to vanilla `num_beams=6` which often returns 6 paraphrases.

related_terms_explained.connection: Diverse beam search is a direct extension of standard beam search to address its mode-collapse problem.
Diverse beam search is the standard way to produce useful n-best lists where the candidates are actually different.
Sampling with temperature is the alternative for getting variety — easier but less controllable in quality.
Minimum Bayes Risk decoding often pairs with diverse beam search as the candidate-generation step.

code_example:
out = model.generate(
    input_ids,
    num_beams=6,
    num_beam_groups=3,
    diversity_penalty=0.7,
    num_return_sequences=6,
)

title: DRY Sampling
short_definition: A sampling penalty that suppresses tokens that would create exact repetitions of recent text — a precision tool for fixing 'the model keeps repeating itself' problems.
full_explanation:
DRY ('Don't Repeat Yourself') sampling, introduced by Pixelibly in 2024 and quickly adopted by llama.cpp and KoboldCpp, addresses a specific failure mode: the model emits the same phrase or sentence two or three times in a row. Standard repetition penalties (which uniformly downweight any previously-seen token) over-penalize common words and underpenalize multi-token repetitions like phrases. DRY targets the actual problem.

The algorithm: at each step, scan the recent context for sequences ending at the current position. For any sequence of length L that would extend a previous occurrence by emitting the next candidate token, multiply that token's probability by `multiplier * base^(L - allowed_length)`. Long matched sequences get crushed; short common ones survive. Default settings might be `multiplier=0.8, base=1.75, allowed_length=2`, meaning two-token overlaps are fine but three-token overlaps get a 0.8x penalty, four get 1.4x, five get 2.45x.

It's especially useful for creative writing, roleplay, and long-form generation where the model otherwise loops on phrases like 'I cannot stress enough how' or repeats stage directions. Most quantized model UIs expose DRY as a slider; OpenAI/Anthropic don't expose it because their post-trained models rarely loop.
why_it_matters: If you've used a smaller open-source model for creative writing, you've seen the loop problem. DRY is the cleanest known solution — a single hyperparameter that surgically kills exact repetitions without harming legitimate echoes (a refrain, a callback, a deliberate motif). For self-hosted writers, roleplayers, and anyone running 7B-13B models for content, DRY is roughly mandatory. It's a good example of an open-source-community-driven sampling improvement that the closed labs simply don't need because their flagship models don't have the underlying problem.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine an editor reading your draft with a highlighter. They circle every phrase that's a near-copy of an earlier phrase. The longer the copy, the harder they cross it out. Short overlaps (two words, common phrases) are fine — language repeats naturally. Long overlaps (a whole sentence appearing twice) get killed. DRY is that editor, applied at every token before the next one is sampled.
see_it_in_practice: In llama.cpp: `--dry-multiplier 0.8 --dry-base 1.75 --dry-allowed-length 2`. Try it on a Llama-3-8B running creative writing — the difference between 'I cannot stress enough...I cannot stress enough...' loops and clean coherent prose is dramatic and immediate.

related_terms_explained.connection: The traditional penalty DRY improves on by being length-aware instead of token-uniform.
OpenAI's API equivalent — applies a uniform penalty to previously-used tokens, less surgical than DRY.
Sister of frequency penalty, applies a flat penalty for any prior occurrence.
DRY pairs well with min-p sampling for quality creative writing on small models.

title: EAGLE Decoding
short_definition: A speculative-decoding variant that uses a tiny draft network — trained on the target model's hidden states — to predict multiple tokens ahead with very high acceptance rates.
full_explanation:
Standard speculative decoding pairs a big target model with a separate small draft model (e.g., 70B + 1B). The acceptance rate depends on how well the small model approximates the big one — usually 50-70% for generic pairs. EAGLE (Extrapolation Algorithm for Greater Language-model Efficiency) replaces the draft model with a small autoregressive head trained directly on the target model's intermediate hidden states. Because it learns the target's distribution rather than approximating it, acceptance rates climb to 80-90%.

The architecture is one transformer decoder layer (~50M params) that takes the target model's last-layer hidden state and predicts the next token's hidden state, autoregressively, for several steps. Those predicted hidden states get projected through the target's lm_head to produce draft tokens. Verification then runs once through the full target model. EAGLE-2 (2024) added dynamic tree drafting — instead of one linear chain of drafts, it explores multiple branches and accepts the longest verified path.

Real-world speedups: EAGLE-2 reports 3-4x throughput on Llama-3-70B vs vanilla decoding, with bit-identical output. Implementations exist in vLLM, SGLang, and the SafeAILab reference repo. Pre-trained EAGLE heads ship for popular base models (Llama, Mistral, Qwen).
why_it_matters: For anyone running a self-hosted inference cluster, EAGLE turns the per-token cost down by a factor of 3-4 with zero quality change. That's the single biggest 'free' efficiency win available in 2025 short of switching to a smaller model. Open-source labs have leaned into it because it's one of the few places where the open-weight ecosystem can match closed-API serving economics. If you're running a vLLM or SGLang fleet for anything beyond hobby scale, you should know about EAGLE — and if you're not running it yet, it's almost always worth the integration time.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of it as building a custom autocomplete for one specific person. Generic autocomplete (regular speculative decoding) catches obvious patterns. But if you trained an autocomplete on years of one person's writing, it would predict their phrasing nearly perfectly — most suggestions accepted, very few rejections. EAGLE is that personalized autocomplete: the draft network is custom-trained for one specific target model's quirks.
see_it_in_practice: In vLLM: `--speculative-model SafeAILab/EAGLE-LLaMA3-Instruct-70B --num-speculative-tokens 5 --use-eagle`. Pre-trained EAGLE heads for major models live at github.com/SafeAILab/EAGLE.

related_terms_explained.connection: EAGLE is a specialized speculative decoding variant with much higher acceptance rates.
EAGLE is the most effective form of assistant-guided decoding currently in production.
An alternative speculative-decoding approach using multiple prediction heads on the target model itself.
EAGLE decoding requires careful KV cache rollback when drafts are rejected.

title: Entropy-Based Decoding
short_definition: A decoding strategy that adjusts how randomly the model generates based on how confident it is at each step — sampling broadly when uncertain, narrowly when sure.
full_explanation:
Standard sampling uses a fixed temperature for every token. But the model's certainty varies: predicting `the` after `I went to` is high-confidence, while predicting the next word in 'I love' is wildly uncertain. Entropy-based decoding (a family that includes entropix and adaptive temperature schemes) reads the entropy of the next-token distribution and adapts. High entropy (model unsure) → sample more aggressively, possibly even injecting a chain-of-thought prompt. Low entropy (model confident) → essentially greedy.

The entropix project popularized this in 2024 by combining entropy with 'varentropy' (variance of entropy) to detect different uncertainty regimes — confident, uncertain-but-clear, and confused. Each regime triggers different actions: sample normally, branch to chain-of-thought, or pause and re-prompt. The technique extracts measurably better reasoning from existing models without fine-tuning, particularly on math benchmarks.

Production adoption is still nascent. The infrastructure cost (extra logging, branching logic) means most serving systems stick with fixed sampling. But the research direction is influential — it's part of a broader move toward 'inference-time compute' where you spend more compute per token when the model needs it.
why_it_matters: Inference-time compute is one of the dominant 2025 themes — OpenAI's o1 family popularized 'thinking longer makes models smarter,' and entropy-based decoding is one open-source path to similar gains without retraining. If you're running open models and want to push reasoning quality without fine-tuning, this is a research direction worth tracking. The downside is that current implementations are research-grade and not turnkey, so it's more 'know about it' than 'deploy it' for most teams today.
how_to_think_about_it: Picture a driver on a familiar route versus an unfamiliar one. On the familiar road, they're confident and drive efficiently — no second-guessing turns. In a new neighborhood, they slow down, look at the GPS, maybe try alternate routes. Entropy decoding is the model doing exactly this: cruise at greedy speed when certain, slow down and explore when not. The hard part is detecting the 'unfamiliar' regime cleanly — confidence is a tricky signal.
see_it_in_practice: The reference implementation is at github.com/xjdr-alt/entropix. Best paired with reasoning-heavy benchmarks (MATH, GSM8K) where the entropy signal is most informative.

related_terms_explained.connection: Entropy decoding is essentially adaptive temperature, varying with model confidence.
Both are measures of model uncertainty; entropy decoding uses it as a real-time control signal.
Entropy decoding can trigger CoT branching when uncertainty is high.
Entropy decoding is one technique under the broader 'spend more compute when needed' umbrella.

title: Frequency Penalty
short_definition: A sampling parameter that subtracts a small amount from a token's logit each time it has already appeared — making the model less likely to use the same word over and over.
full_explanation:
LLMs sometimes get stuck repeating phrases or words, especially with low temperature or in long generations. Frequency penalty adds a counter-pressure: for each token at sampling time, subtract `penalty * count_in_output` from its logit. A token used five times already gets penalized 5x. Tokens never used get no penalty. This is purely linear in count.

The practical effect is gentle suppression of overused vocabulary while leaving fresh tokens untouched. Common settings are 0.0-2.0; OpenAI, Anthropic, and most open-weight servers expose it. It's distinct from presence penalty (which applies a flat penalty for any prior use, regardless of count) and from repetition penalty (which multiplies probabilities instead of subtracting from logits).

Useful in: long-form writing where you want vocabulary variety, list generation where the model would otherwise repeat list items, and creative tasks where genericness comes from word reuse. Less useful in: structured output, code generation, or anything where exact tokens are intentionally repeated (function names, type signatures, JSON keys all get hurt by this).
why_it_matters: One of the easiest knobs to turn when output feels repetitive. Costs nothing, ships in every API, takes seconds to test. For practitioners shipping content generation, summarization, or long-form chat features, knowing when to bump frequency_penalty from 0 to 0.3 separates outputs that feel mechanical from outputs that feel written. Doesn't replace good prompting or model choice but smooths over a real failure mode at near-zero cost.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of it like a small tax that increases each time you use a word. The first 'innovation' is free. The fifth 'innovation' costs you. The tax is small enough that if 'innovation' is genuinely the right word, you'll still pay it — but if any synonym would do, the tax pushes you there. The opposite-of-default is frequency_penalty=2, which makes outputs read like a thesaurus exploded.
see_it_in_practice: Try this experiment: ask GPT-4 to 'list 20 reasons to learn Python' with `frequency_penalty=0` then with `frequency_penalty=0.6`. The first will repeat 'powerful', 'versatile', 'easy' across many entries; the second will reach for fresh adjectives. Same prompt, different output character.

related_terms_explained.connection: Sister parameter — flat penalty for any prior use, while frequency penalty scales with count.
Older multiplicative version that applies similarly but with different math.
Modern phrase-level repetition penalty that's more surgical than frequency penalty.
Frequency penalty is essentially per-token logit bias that scales with usage count.

code_example:
client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=msgs,
    frequency_penalty=0.5,  # gentle anti-repetition
)

title: Greedy Decoding
short_definition: The simplest way to generate text from a language model: at every step, just pick the single most likely next word.
full_explanation:
After running a forward pass, an LLM produces a probability distribution over its entire vocabulary — typically 32K-200K tokens, each with a score. Greedy decoding picks the highest-scored token, appends it, and repeats. No randomness, no exploration, no second chances. The output is fully deterministic for a given input and weights.

Greedy is the cheapest decoder (no sampling math, no parallel beams) and the simplest to reason about. It's what you want for: testing model behavior under controlled conditions, evaluating accuracy on tasks with one right answer, and any setting where reproducibility matters more than diversity. It's what you don't want for: open-ended generation (output gets generic and repetitive fast), creative writing (every story sounds the same), and anything where multiple valid answers exist.

When you set `temperature=0` in an API call, you're effectively asking for greedy decoding. Production systems often default to greedy for evaluation pipelines and switch to sampling for user-facing generation. The trap: greedy can get stuck in loops — 'I am happy. I am happy. I am happy.' — because the most-likely token at every position chains into a degenerate sequence.
why_it_matters: Greedy is the baseline every other decoding strategy is measured against. Beam search is greedy with parallel branches; sampling is greedy with controlled randomness; speculative decoding still verifies against greedy as ground truth. If you don't understand greedy, you can't reason about why temperature changes output, why your eval scores swing run-to-run, or why your chatbot occasionally loops. It's the simplest concept in LLM serving and the foundation of everything else.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine reading a choose-your-own-adventure book and at every choice always picking the option that 'sounds best right now,' with no thought to where the path leads. You'll move fast and never agonize, but you'll often get a boring or trapped ending. Greedy decoding is exactly that — locally optimal, sometimes globally terrible. The fix is one of: explore alternatives (beam search), accept some randomness (sampling), or look ahead (speculative variants).
see_it_in_practice: In any HuggingFace model: `model.generate(input_ids, do_sample=False)` is greedy. In OpenAI API: `temperature=0`. Run the same prompt twice with these settings — output should be byte-identical. If it isn't, you've found a non-determinism bug worth investigating.

related_terms_explained.connection: Beam search is greedy with N parallel candidates kept alive instead of just one.
Temperature 0 reduces sampling to greedy decoding.
Greedy is the default deterministic decoding strategy.
Top-k sampling is greedy with controlled randomness over the top K candidates.

code_example: out = model.generate(input_ids, do_sample=False, max_new_tokens=100)

title: Guidance (Microsoft)
short_definition: A Microsoft-built library that lets you write generation prompts as templates with embedded constraints, regex, and control flow — and runs them with constrained decoding under the hood.
full_explanation:
Guidance treats prompts as small programs. You write a template like `My name is {{select 'name' options=['Alice','Bob','Charlie']}} and I am {{gen 'age' regex='[0-9]+'}} years old`, and Guidance compiles that into a sequence of constrained-decoding operations: at the `select` it allows only the listed options; at the `gen` with regex it allows only matching tokens. The model fills in valid completions; Guidance handles the constraint compilation.

The library predates the JSON-mode APIs from OpenAI and Anthropic and pioneered many of the patterns those APIs adopted. Microsoft Research released it in 2023 alongside the Phi model series. It supports: regex-constrained generation, choice selection, multi-token completions, programmatic looping, and custom grammars. It works with both local models (via Transformers, llama.cpp) and OpenAI's API (with reduced functionality, since OpenAI doesn't expose token-level constraints).

The killer use case: agents and structured workflows where you need a sequence of generations, each with different constraints — first a selection from 5 options, then a regex-validated number, then a freeform paragraph. Doing this manually with prompt engineering is brittle; Guidance handles it as a single executable template.
why_it_matters: If you're building agents or structured pipelines on local models and find yourself writing 'please respond in JSON' prompts and parsing failures, Guidance is the cleaner tool. It's been somewhat overshadowed by Outlines (which has a faster FSM compiler and better HuggingFace integration) and by tool-use APIs from closed providers, but it remains the most expressive option for templated multi-step generation. Worth knowing about, especially if you're working with smaller open models where reliability is harder to come by.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of Guidance like a fill-in-the-blank form, except the blanks have rules. Some blanks are 'pick one of these.' Some are 'must match this pattern.' Some are 'free text up to 100 words.' The model fills the blanks; Guidance enforces the rules at the token level. Compared to writing 'please respond in JSON like {...}' and hoping, this is the difference between a parser and a prayer.
see_it_in_practice: On GitHub at github.com/guidance-ai/guidance. Most useful when paired with smaller models (3B-13B) where output reliability is shaky — Guidance turns 90%-reliable models into 100%-reliable structured-output systems for the constrained portions of your pipeline.

related_terms_explained.connection: Guidance is a high-level interface for constrained decoding with template syntax.
Competing library with similar goals — Outlines is faster, Guidance more expressive.
Guidance is one of the main tools for reliable structured output from local models.
Guidance generalizes prompt templates with embedded constraint syntax.

code_example:
import guidance
lm = guidance.models.LlamaCpp('llama-3-8b.gguf')
lm += f'My favorite color is {guidance.select(["red","blue","green"])}'
print(lm['favorite color'])

title: Logit Bias
short_definition: A sampling parameter that lets you push specific tokens up or down in the model's output distribution by adding a fixed value to their logits before sampling.
full_explanation:
Every token in the vocabulary has a logit at each generation step — a raw score before softmax converts it to probability. Logit bias lets you intervene: add +5 to a token's logit and it becomes much more likely; subtract 100 and it becomes effectively forbidden. The bias is applied uniformly at every step regardless of context.

Classic uses: forbid specific words ('moist', 'unfortunately'), force a yes/no answer (massively bias 'yes' and 'no', nothing else), bias toward technical vocabulary, or implement a content filter at the sampling layer. OpenAI exposes it via the `logit_bias` parameter on chat completions (limited to 300 token-bias pairs per call); HuggingFace exposes it via custom LogitsProcessors with no limits.

The gotcha: token boundaries matter. The word 'cat' might tokenize to one token or two depending on the surrounding context (' cat' vs 'cat' vs 'Cat'). To reliably bias 'cat' you need to bias all relevant token IDs. Tools like the OpenAI Tokenizer playground are essential for finding the right IDs. Misuse — biasing common subwords without thinking — produces garbled output.
why_it_matters: Logit bias is the simplest precision tool for shaping LLM output. It's faster than prompt engineering ('please don't use the word X') because it operates at the token level with mathematical certainty rather than asking the model to follow instructions. For specific use cases — yes/no classifiers, vocabulary control, mandatory format tokens — it's the right tool. For broad behavioral changes (tone, style), it's the wrong tool. Knowing the difference saves a lot of debugging time.
how_to_think_about_it: Logit bias is like a small electromagnet that pulls the model toward or away from specific words. It doesn't argue; it doesn't request; it just changes the probabilities. Useful when you have a list of token IDs and a specific direction in mind. Useless for anything fuzzy like 'be more formal.' The bigger the bias magnitude, the stronger the magnet — at +100, the model essentially has no choice but that token if it's even a plausible candidate.
see_it_in_practice: Build a yes/no classifier in two lines: tokenize 'yes' and 'no', set logit_bias to {'yes_id': 100, 'no_id': 100}, set max_tokens=1. The model can only output yes or no — perfect for binary classification at API speed. This pattern is widely used in production LLM-as-judge pipelines.

related_terms_explained.connection: Logit bias modifies logits before they become logprobs at sampling time.
Logit bias is the simplest manual form of constrained decoding.
Logit bias and stop sequences both shape output; bias controls likelihood, stop sequences control length.
Effective logit bias requires understanding which tokens encode the words you care about.

code_example:
# Force yes/no classifier with OpenAI
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=[{'role':'user','content': prompt}],
    max_tokens=1,
    logit_bias={9891: 100, 2201: 100}  # 'yes', 'no' token IDs
)

title: Log Probability (Logprobs)
short_definition: The natural log of how likely the model thought each generated token was — useful for confidence scoring, alternative-token analysis, and detecting model uncertainty.
full_explanation:
Every token an LLM generates comes with an underlying probability. APIs return these as logarithms (typically natural log) because raw probabilities can be tiny floats (1e-20) that lose precision. A logprob of -0.1 means the model was 90% confident; -2.3 means about 10%; -10 means about 0.005%.

OpenAI, Anthropic, and most open serving stacks expose two logprob views: the chosen token's logprob, and optionally the top-K alternative tokens with their logprobs. From these you can reconstruct: how confident the model was at each step, which alternatives it considered, where in the response confidence dropped sharply (often coinciding with hallucination boundaries), and whether the model is exploring a generic or unusual continuation.

Production uses: building confidence scores for downstream filtering, computing perplexity offline, detecting uncertainty regimes for entropy-based decoding, and constructing semantic-similarity scores between generated and reference text. Logprobs also power most automated jailbreak defenses — sudden confidence drops at certain token positions often signal that the model is being pushed off-distribution.
why_it_matters: If you're building anything that needs to know 'how sure was the model about that answer,' logprobs are the only honest signal. Self-reported confidence ('I'm 95% sure') is unreliable because the model has no introspection into its own probabilities; logprobs do. For evals, eval pipelines, semantic search via LLM-scored matching, and any production system that needs uncertainty quantification, logprobs are essential. Open-source serving (vLLM, TGI) supports them. Closed APIs charge slightly extra (or limit them) because returning top-K logprobs bloats response size — but they're available everywhere that matters.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of logprob as the model showing its work. After producing each token, it tells you not just what it picked but how confident it was — and optionally what its second and third choices were. A confident model produces a string of values close to 0 (high probability). An uncertain model produces a string of large negative numbers and lots of close-call alternatives. Tracking the curve along a generation is like watching an EKG — flat when the model is on safe ground, spiky when it's improvising.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI: `logprobs=True, top_logprobs=5` in chat completions returns the top 5 alternatives at every position. Plot the chosen-token logprob across a response and you'll see exactly where the model 'guessed' versus where it was confident — incredibly useful for hallucination detection.

related_terms_explained.connection: Perplexity is computed directly from logprobs — the exponential of the average negative logprob.
Logit bias modifies the underlying logits whose softmax produces the probabilities behind logprobs.
Temperature reshapes the probability distribution; logprobs reflect the resulting distribution.
Entropy decoding uses logprob distributions at each step to pick decoding strategy.

code_example:
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=msgs,
    logprobs=True,
    top_logprobs=5,
)
for token in resp.choices[0].logprobs.content:
    print(token.token, token.logprob, token.top_logprobs)

title: Min-P Sampling
short_definition: A sampling cutoff that scales with the model's confidence: keep only tokens whose probability is at least P fraction of the most-likely token's probability.
full_explanation:
Top-p (nucleus) sampling has a known weakness: in low-confidence states, even the cumulative top 90% can include junk tokens. Min-p (Nguyen et al., 2023) fixes this with a relative threshold. If the highest-probability token has probability 0.5 and you set min_p=0.1, you keep all tokens with probability ≥ 0.05 (10% of the top). If the top token only has probability 0.05 (high uncertainty), you keep all tokens with probability ≥ 0.005 — a much smaller fraction of the distribution but the right set.

This adapts to confidence in a way top-p doesn't. When the model is confident, min-p collapses to a small set of similar candidates (clean output). When uncertain, it widens to allow exploration without including obvious garbage. Empirically, min-p produces noticeably better creative writing on small open-source models than top-p with comparable hyperparameters, and it's robust to wide temperature ranges.

llama.cpp added it in late 2023 and most quantized model UIs (KoboldCpp, text-generation-webui, oobabooga) expose it. Hugging Face supports it via the `min_p` parameter in `generate()`. Closed APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic) don't expose it because their post-trained models work fine with top-p.
why_it_matters: For self-hosters running 7B-13B models, min-p is the single most reliable sampling improvement available. The community converged on it through testing, not papers — every recommendation thread for local model writing setups in 2024-2025 mentions it. If you're using top-p and getting either generic or chaotic output depending on the prompt, switching to min-p with a value of 0.05-0.1 typically fixes both at once. It's the kind of small technical detail that materially changes the output quality of self-hosted models.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of top-p as 'keep enough candidates to add up to 90% of probability mass' — fixed cumulative. Min-p is 'keep all candidates that are at least 10% as likely as the top one' — relative to the leader. The relative threshold automatically widens when the leader is uncertain and narrows when the leader is confident. Top-p applies the same rule in both cases and gets it wrong half the time.
see_it_in_practice: In llama.cpp: `--min-p 0.05`. In HuggingFace: `model.generate(input_ids, do_sample=True, min_p=0.05, temperature=1.0)`. Pair with temperature 1.0-1.5 for creative writing — min-p handles the cutoff, temperature handles the spread.

related_terms_explained.connection: Min-p directly addresses top-p's failure mode in low-confidence states.
Both filter the candidate pool — top-k by count, min-p by relative probability.
Min-p is robust to temperature variation in a way top-p isn't.
Min-p pairs well with DRY for creative writing on small models.

code_example:
out = model.generate(
    input_ids,
    do_sample=True,
    min_p=0.05,
    temperature=1.0,
    max_new_tokens=300,
)

title: Mirostat
short_definition: A sampling algorithm that targets a specific perplexity (model surprise) by dynamically adjusting the cutoff at every step — keeping outputs at a steady level of creativity.
full_explanation:
Standard sampling parameters (top-p, temperature) set static thresholds; the actual perplexity of the output drifts based on the model's local confidence. Mirostat (Basu et al., 2020) inverts this: you specify the target perplexity (the 'tau' parameter, typically 5-7), and the algorithm adjusts the effective top-k at every step to maintain it. High local confidence → cutoff opens up to maintain target surprise; low confidence → cutoff tightens to filter out garbage.

The result is more consistent output character. Standard top-p creative writing has stretches of generic prose punctuated by chaotic detours; mirostat smooths this into steady-flow text at whatever surprise level you choose. Tau=3 produces conservative academic-style writing; tau=7 produces flowing creative prose; tau=10 starts getting weird.

Mirostat 2.0 simplified the original algorithm and is the version shipped in llama.cpp, KoboldCpp, and most quantized model UIs. It's particularly popular for roleplay and long-form fiction because the perplexity target prevents both the 'boring loop' failure and the 'chaotic drift' failure. Rarely used in production API serving where speed matters more than character.
why_it_matters: For self-hosted writers, roleplayers, and anyone optimizing for output consistency in long generations, mirostat is a credible alternative to the top-p+temperature dance. The mental model — 'I want this much surprise per token, hold it steady' — maps well to creative goals. Less universally adopted than min-p (which is simpler and faster), but for long-form work where output character drift matters, it's worth testing. Like most sampling-side improvements, it's an open-source-community contribution that closed APIs don't offer.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of cruise control on a car. Without it, you press the gas and your speed drifts based on hills and headwind. With it, you pick a target speed and the car constantly adjusts throttle to maintain it. Mirostat is cruise control for output surprise — pick a target perplexity, and the algorithm keeps every token at roughly that level of creativity. The tradeoff: cruise control feels less responsive than active driving. Some creative writers prefer the unevenness of standard sampling.
see_it_in_practice: In llama.cpp: `--mirostat 2 --mirostat-tau 5.0 --mirostat-eta 0.1`. The eta controls how aggressively the cutoff adapts. Higher eta = more responsive (more drift); lower = smoother (more lag). Start with defaults and adjust.

related_terms_explained.connection: Mirostat targets a specific perplexity value as its control objective.
Mirostat is an alternative to top-p with adaptive rather than fixed cutoffs.
Mirostat replaces the temperature+top-p combo with a single perplexity target.
Both are adaptive cutoff strategies; min-p uses relative probability, mirostat targets perplexity.

title: Multinomial Sampling
short_definition: The default randomized sampling strategy: at each step, draw a token from the model's full probability distribution like rolling a weighted die.
full_explanation:
Multinomial sampling is the simplest stochastic decoder. Take the model's softmax-normalized probability distribution over the vocabulary, treat it as a weighted lottery, and draw one token. Higher-probability tokens win more often, but every token with non-zero probability has a chance of being picked. No truncation, no cutoff — every token in the vocabulary is in play.

In raw form this is rarely used directly because long-tail tokens (probability 1e-7) occasionally get drawn and produce garbage. In practice, multinomial sampling is always combined with at least one filter: temperature scaling, top-k, top-p, min-p, or some combination. The combined chain is what most APIs call 'sampling' or 'do_sample=True' in HuggingFace.

Understanding multinomial as the underlying primitive matters because all the higher-level techniques (top-k, top-p, etc.) just modify the distribution before multinomial draws from it. Top-k zeroes out everything outside the top K. Top-p zeroes out the long tail beyond cumulative P. Temperature reshapes the distribution shape. The actual sampling step is always the same multinomial draw.
why_it_matters: If you don't understand the distinction between 'multinomial sampling' (the math) and 'top-p sampling' (a particular distribution shaping before multinomial), the API parameters get confusing fast. Once you see that everything reduces to 'modify the distribution, then draw,' the parameters become orthogonal levers — temperature shapes spread, top-p limits tail, frequency penalty discourages reuse, min-p sets relative floor, and so on. Each parameter can be reasoned about independently.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of the model's output distribution as a giant pizza, where each slice is a possible next token sized by probability. Multinomial sampling is throwing a dart blindfolded: where it lands is your token. Big slices get hit more often, tiny slices rarely. All the other sampling tricks are about reshaping the pizza before the throw — cutting off bad slices, scaling slice sizes, removing the long tail of crumbs. The throw itself is always the same.
see_it_in_practice: Pure multinomial without filters is `model.generate(input_ids, do_sample=True, temperature=1.0, top_k=0, top_p=1.0)`. Try this on a small model — output usually degrades into nonsense within a few sentences as the long tail bleeds in. That's why all production sampling stacks add at least one filter.

related_terms_explained.connection: Temperature is the most common reshaping applied before multinomial draws.
Top-k filters the distribution to the K most likely tokens before multinomial sampling.
Top-p filters by cumulative probability mass before multinomial sampling.
Greedy is multinomial sampling at temperature=0 — the distribution becomes a delta function.

title: Outlines (Structured LLM)
short_definition: An open-source Python library that forces local LLMs to produce valid JSON, regex patterns, or grammars by compiling them to finite-state machines and masking invalid tokens at sampling time.
full_explanation:
Outlines (formerly normal-computing/outlines) emerged in 2023 as the most-used open-source library for constrained decoding. You hand it a Pydantic model, a regex, or an EBNF grammar; it compiles to a finite-state machine; at every generation step, the FSM exposes the set of token IDs that would maintain a valid prefix. Outlines masks all other tokens to negative infinity in the logits, then samples normally from what remains.

The core insight is precomputation. Compiling a JSON schema to an FSM is expensive (one-time) but querying 'which tokens are valid here' becomes O(1) lookup. After the upfront cost, constrained generation runs at near-normal sampling speed. Outlines integrates with Transformers, llama.cpp, vLLM, and MLX — the same constraint compiles once and runs across multiple backends.

It powers production systems where reliability matters more than novelty: structured data extraction, function calling on local models, and any pipeline where 'parse failure' would mean a customer-visible bug. The team also published research on the underlying FSM-compilation algorithms, which became influential — most newer constrained-decoding implementations (LM Format Enforcer, structured-decoding modes in vLLM) trace back to Outlines' approach.
why_it_matters: Outlines is one of the standout open-source contributions to LLM production tooling. Before it, structured output from local models was 'prompt and pray.' Now it's a guaranteed parse. For self-hosters running open models in production, Outlines turns the model into a reliable JSON-or-anything-else producer with no fine-tuning. Closed APIs caught up later with built-in JSON modes, but Outlines remains the most flexible — it handles arbitrary regex and grammars, not just JSON.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of it as a constraint compiler that turns 'must be valid JSON matching this schema' into a runtime token-by-token allowlist. Every step, the compiler tells the sampler: 'these 47 tokens are legal, the other 199,953 are not.' The model picks among the 47. The result is mathematically guaranteed to satisfy the structural constraints — not a model promise, an arithmetic fact.
see_it_in_practice: GitHub: github.com/outlines-dev/outlines. Five-line example: `import outlines; m = outlines.models.transformers('Qwen/Qwen2.5-1.5B'); g = outlines.generate.json(m, MyPydanticSchema); result = g('extract from: ...')`. Returns a validated Python object every time, including on tiny 1.5B models that would otherwise produce 30% malformed JSON.

related_terms_explained.connection: Outlines is the leading open-source implementation of constrained decoding.
Outlines is one of the primary tools for getting reliable structured output from local LLMs.
Microsoft's competing library with similar goals — Outlines is faster, Guidance more expressive.
Outlines can implement function calling on any local model, not just those with API-side support.

code_example:
from pydantic import BaseModel
import outlines

class Person(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int
    email: str

model = outlines.models.transformers('Qwen/Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct')
generator = outlines.generate.json(model, Person)
person = generator('Extract: Jane Doe, 28, jane@example.com')

title: Perplexity
short_definition: A measure of how surprised a language model is by a piece of text — lower means the model finds the text more predictable, higher means more surprising.
full_explanation:
Perplexity is the geometric mean of the inverse probabilities a model assigned to each token in a sequence. Mathematically: `perplexity = exp(-1/N * sum(log P(token_i)))`. Equivalently, it's `2^(cross-entropy)` for a base-2 system. A perplexity of 10 means the model was, on average, choosing among 10 equally-likely options at each token. Perplexity of 100 means 100 — the model was less sure.

Three common uses:

1. **Model evaluation.** Lower perplexity on held-out test text usually correlates with better generation quality. Llama-3-70B has lower perplexity on web text than Llama-3-8B, which corresponds to its better generation quality. Used as the standard pre-training metric.

2. **Quantization comparison.** Quantizing Llama-3-8B to 4-bit raises perplexity from ~6.0 to ~6.4 — a small bump that translates to barely-noticeable quality loss. Perplexity is the de facto comparison metric for quantization aggressiveness.

3. **Decoding control.** Mirostat sampling targets a specific perplexity. Some text-classification systems use perplexity gaps as 'this text is likely AI-generated' signals.

Perplexity is *not* a general quality metric for generation — a model that produces 'the the the the' has very low perplexity but terrible quality. It measures predictability, not goodness.
why_it_matters: Perplexity is the universal language for comparing model fits to text — it's how every paper compares quantization methods, how every pretraining run reports progress, and how most decoding-control tricks ground their parameters. If you don't know what your model's perplexity is on representative text, you can't reason quantitatively about quantization tradeoffs, fine-tuning effects, or decoding-strategy choices. It's a foundational metric that pays back the day you spend learning it.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine reading a sentence one word at a time and trying to guess each next word. Perplexity is the average size of the set of possibilities you considered before peeking. Low perplexity (close to 1) means you nearly always guessed right — the text is predictable to you. High perplexity (10+) means at every word you were considering many options. The model 'reads' text the same way; perplexity quantifies how surprising it found it.
see_it_in_practice: Common workflow: `perplexity = exp(loss)` after running model evaluation on a held-out corpus like WikiText. For quantization comparison: quantize your model two different ways, measure perplexity on the same text, the lower-perplexity quant is usually the better one. The gap rarely exceeds 0.5-1.0 between sane quantization options.

related_terms_explained.connection: Perplexity is computed directly from logprobs — exp of negative average logprob.
Perplexity is the standard metric for comparing quantization aggressiveness.
Mirostat sampling targets a specific perplexity value during generation.
Perplexity is the exponential of cross-entropy loss; same information, different units.

code_example:
import torch, math
# Per-batch perplexity on a held-out corpus
with torch.no_grad():
    loss = model(input_ids, labels=input_ids).loss
ppl = math.exp(loss.item())

title: Presence Penalty
short_definition: A sampling parameter that subtracts a flat amount from a token's logit if it has appeared at all in the output — encouraging the model to introduce new vocabulary.
full_explanation:
Presence penalty is the discrete sibling of frequency penalty. While frequency penalty scales with how many times a token has been used (linear), presence penalty applies a flat discount the moment a token shows up at all (binary). A token used once gets the same penalty as a token used ten times.

Mechanics: at each step, for every token T in the previously-generated output, subtract `presence_penalty` from T's logit. Range typically 0.0-2.0. The cumulative effect: tokens already used become less likely; unused tokens are unaffected. This nudges the model toward exploration.

When to use which: presence penalty is right when you want the model to introduce new ideas/vocabulary across a long generation (essays, brainstorms, lists). Frequency penalty is right when you want to suppress excessive repetition of common words (think: 'the', 'and') in long-form text. They can be combined — small values of each often work better than large values of either.

Like frequency penalty, presence penalty is exposed in OpenAI, Anthropic, and most open-weight serving APIs. Same caveat: too high and you get thesaurus-disease (the model reaches for unusual synonyms even when normal ones are right).
why_it_matters: Useful for: brainstorms ('list 50 ideas for...'), long-form essays, content generation that should not feel formulaic. The signature failure mode it solves is 'the model keeps coming back to the same handful of words.' Presence penalty pushes outward; frequency penalty pulls down repeats. Both are cheap, ship in every API, and worth a 5-minute experiment when output feels stale.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine a writer with a list of words they've already used in this draft. With presence penalty, every time they consider one of those words again, there's a small mental tax — a slight push toward picking something new. The tax is the same whether the word was used once or twenty times. Compared to frequency penalty (where the tax compounds with use), presence penalty is gentler on common short words but stronger on first-time-only filtering.
see_it_in_practice: Generate 'a list of 30 startup ideas' first with `presence_penalty=0`, then `presence_penalty=1.0`. The first will repeat 'AI-powered platform for X' across many entries. The second will reach for fresh framings — 'subscription service', 'community-driven', 'B2B marketplace' instead of looping.

related_terms_explained.connection: Sister parameter — frequency scales with use count, presence is flat-once-used.
Older multiplicative version that combines elements of both.
Presence penalty is essentially a dynamic per-token logit bias triggered by usage.
DRY is a more sophisticated phrase-level alternative for fighting longer repetition patterns.

code_example:
client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=msgs,
    presence_penalty=0.6,
)

title: Reasoning Token / Thinking Budget
short_definition: The cap on how many internal 'thinking' tokens a reasoning-trained model (like o1 or DeepSeek-R1) can spend before producing its final answer.
full_explanation:
Reasoning models like OpenAI o1, DeepSeek-R1, and Qwen QwQ produce a hidden chain of thought before answering. These internal tokens — sometimes thousands of them — let the model work through a problem step by step, but they cost compute and money. The reasoning_token_budget caps how many internal tokens the model is allowed to spend.

Mechanically, the model has a separate token slot reserved for thinking. With a budget of 1,000 reasoning tokens and 200 visible-output tokens, the model can think up to 1,000 tokens before it must commit to an answer. If the budget is exhausted mid-thought, the model is forced to wrap up. Higher budgets typically improve accuracy on hard problems (math, code, multi-step planning) and waste money on easy ones.

OpenAI's o1 family exposes this as `max_completion_tokens` (with reasoning tokens counted internally and billed separately). DeepSeek-R1 exposes raw reasoning. Anthropic's Claude with extended thinking exposes a `thinking.budget_tokens` parameter. Each provider has its own knob name but the concept is identical: pay more compute for more reasoning depth, set a ceiling to control cost.
why_it_matters: Reasoning models are the dominant 2024-2025 pattern for hard tasks, and the budget knob is the most important hyperparameter for using them economically. Setting the budget too low caps quality on hard problems; setting it too high wastes money on easy ones. Most teams find the right answer is task-specific routing: easy queries go to a small model with no reasoning; hard ones get budget=10000 on a reasoning model. For open-source teams running DeepSeek-R1 or QwQ locally, knowing this knob is essential because local compute isn't free either.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of it like a chess clock. The model gets X seconds to think before it must move. With a generous clock, it can consider variations, double-check, and pick the best response. With a tight clock, it plays the first reasonable move. The right clock depends on the position: simple endgame, no thinking needed; complex middlegame, lots of thinking pays. Reasoning budget is the same control applied to LLMs, with the cost of 'thinking time' being literal compute dollars.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI o1: `client.chat.completions.create(model='o1-mini', messages=msgs, max_completion_tokens=4000)` where the model uses up to most of that for reasoning. Anthropic Claude with thinking: pass `thinking={'budget_tokens': 8000}`. For DeepSeek-R1 served locally, the budget is enforced by your `max_tokens` setting at the inference framework level.

related_terms_explained.connection: Reasoning tokens are essentially internal chain-of-thought, generated by reasoning-trained models.
Reasoning budget is the primary lever for trading inference compute against quality.
Synonym used in research for the same concept of spending more compute at inference.
OpenAI's first model family designed around the reasoning-token-budget paradigm.

code_example:
# Anthropic Claude with extended thinking
resp = client.messages.create(
    model='claude-opus-4-7',
    max_tokens=4096,
    thinking={'type':'enabled','budget_tokens': 10000},
    messages=[{'role':'user','content': hard_problem}]
)

title: Repetition Penalty
short_definition: An old-school sampling parameter that multiplies a token's probability down each time it has appeared, used to stop models from looping on the same words.
full_explanation:
The original recipe for fighting LLM repetition. At each generation step, scan the output so far. For every token that appears in the output, divide its logit by `repetition_penalty` (or multiply by `1/penalty` depending on implementation). Default 1.0 = no effect; 1.1-1.3 = noticeable suppression; 2.0+ = aggressive.

Introduced by CTRL (Keskar et al., 2019), repetition penalty became standard in HuggingFace, llama.cpp, and most open serving stacks. It's distinct from OpenAI's frequency_penalty (which subtracts from logits, scaled by count) and presence_penalty (flat subtraction once used). The multiplicative form interacts non-linearly with temperature — at low temperature, the same penalty has bigger effect.

Its main weakness is that it penalizes tokens uniformly regardless of context. A code generation task naturally repeats `if`, `def`, `return`; a structured-output task repeats keys. Aggressive repetition penalty hurts these tasks. It's been largely superseded for serious work by DRY sampling (which targets phrase-level repeats specifically) and frequency_penalty (which is more interpretable). But it remains widely used, especially in older inference UIs and llama.cpp defaults.
why_it_matters: Still the default first-knob-to-turn for 'why is the model looping' on local models. Easy to understand, ships everywhere, modest values (1.1-1.2) rarely cause harm and often help. For hobbyist generation and one-off content, it's fine. For production or anything where output character matters, learn DRY and frequency_penalty as more precise alternatives. The older the inference stack, the more likely repetition_penalty is the only knob available.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of it like a dimmer switch on every word you've already used. The first time the model says 'happy', the bulb is at 100%. After it says 'happy' once, that bulb dims to maybe 80%. After twice, 64%. After three times, 51%. Eventually the model sees 'happy' as so dim it picks 'joyful' or 'glad' instead. The dimming is the same for every used word, which is fine for prose and bad for code.
see_it_in_practice: In llama.cpp: `--repeat-penalty 1.15`. In HuggingFace: `model.generate(input_ids, repetition_penalty=1.15)`. Start at 1.0, bump to 1.1, then 1.15, then 1.2 — past 1.2 quality often degrades. If you need more aggressive anti-repetition, add DRY sampling instead of pushing repetition_penalty higher.

related_terms_explained.connection: OpenAI's modern subtractive equivalent — same goal, additive math, more interpretable.
Sister of frequency penalty; binary trigger on first use.
Modern phrase-level alternative — more surgical for fighting loops.
Hard ban on n-gram repetition; complementary to soft penalty.

code_example:
out = model.generate(
    input_ids,
    do_sample=True,
    repetition_penalty=1.15,
    temperature=0.8,
)

title: Stop Sequences
short_definition: A list of strings the model checks for after each generated token — the moment it would emit one of them, generation halts immediately.
full_explanation:
Stop sequences let you control exactly where generation ends without trusting the model to know. You provide a list of strings; after every token, the serving stack appends it to the output, checks whether any stop sequence is now present at the end, and if so terminates generation. The matched stop sequence is typically not included in the returned text.

Classic uses: '

Human:' to stop at the next user turn in a multi-turn template, '

' to stop at a paragraph break, '```' to stop after a code block, custom delimiters in agent loops. Most APIs accept up to 4-10 stop sequences. They're cheap (just string matching), reliable, and widely supported.

The gotcha is tokenization: a stop sequence like '
Human:' might span a token boundary differently than expected. Some implementations match on text after detokenization (more reliable, slightly slower); others match on token IDs (faster, occasionally misses). Test your stop sequences on edge cases before deploying — particularly the start of a new generation, where the first token might absorb your delimiter.
why_it_matters: Underrated production tool. If you're building any kind of structured pipeline — agents, multi-turn chat, code generation that should stop at the closing ``` — stop sequences are how you avoid wasting tokens on the model running past its natural endpoint. Saves money, reduces latency, prevents downstream parsing of trailing junk. Every serving stack supports them; very few applications use them as much as they should.
how_to_think_about_it: Stop sequences are the equivalent of telling a meeting attendee 'when you say the word "done" we'll know to stop recording.' The recorder doesn't need to understand the meeting; it just listens for the keyword. Same with stop sequences: the model doesn't know it's stopping; the serving layer just halts the moment your delimiter shows up. Useful when the model has a habit of running on past where you want it.
see_it_in_practice:
OpenAI: `stop=['

Human:', '

User:']` halts at the next conversational turn. For code: `stop=['```']` returns just the code block content. For agents: use a unique sentinel like `<END_TURN>` that the prompt instructs the model to emit when done.

related_terms_explained.connection: Stop sequences are the soft endpoint; max_tokens is the hard upper cap.
Both shape generation; bias controls likelihood, stop controls termination.
Stop sequences are commonly used to bound structured output sections.
Chat templates often include implicit stop sequences (the next role's turn marker).

code_example:
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=msgs,
    stop=['\n\nHuman:', '<END>'],
)

title: Temperature
short_definition: A sampling parameter that controls how random LLM output is — low values make the model conservative and predictable; high values make it adventurous and varied.
full_explanation:
Temperature reshapes the model's probability distribution before sampling. Mathematically, divide every logit by the temperature value, then softmax. Temperature = 1.0 leaves the distribution unchanged. Temperature < 1.0 sharpens the distribution (probabilities concentrate on the top tokens). Temperature > 1.0 flattens it (probabilities spread more evenly). At temperature → 0, the distribution becomes a delta on the top token — equivalent to greedy decoding. At temperature → ∞, it becomes uniform — pure random.

In practice: temperature 0.0-0.3 for tasks with one right answer (extraction, classification, code completion), 0.6-0.9 for chat and general writing, 1.0-1.5 for creative writing where you want variety. Above 1.5, output usually degrades into chaos for most models.

Temperature interacts with everything else. Low temperature + top-p does little — the distribution is already concentrated. High temperature + low top-p means the spread is squashed by the top-p filter. Temperature + frequency_penalty lets you have variety without repetition. The combinations are why decoding feels finicky; understanding the underlying math (logit reshaping) makes them predictable.
why_it_matters: Temperature is the single most-used sampling knob in production LLM serving. Wrong setting = wrong output character. Building a research assistant? Temperature 0.2 — you want consistent, reproducible answers. Building a creative writing coach? Temperature 0.9 — you want variety and flair. Building anything in between? Test, measure, pick. The default in most APIs is 1.0, which is rarely the right answer for either end of the spectrum. Five minutes of experimentation pays off forever.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of temperature as how much coffee the model has had. Decaf (temperature 0.2): polite, careful, says the safe thing every time, never surprises you. Espresso (temperature 1.5): wild, makes leaps, sometimes brilliant, sometimes incoherent. The middle (0.7) is normal coffee — alert and engaged but not bouncing off the walls. Pick the dose that matches the task. Job interview? Decaf. Brainstorm? Espresso.
see_it_in_practice: Run the same prompt at temperature 0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 — easy way to build intuition for how it affects your specific use case. For most production work, the sweet spot is 0.5-0.8. For evals, always 0 (or seed for reproducibility). For creative writing, 0.9-1.2 with min-p 0.05.

related_terms_explained.connection: Temperature 0 is equivalent to greedy decoding.
Temperature reshapes the distribution; top-p then truncates the tail.
Same relationship as top-p — temperature shapes, top-k truncates.
Min-p is more robust to temperature variation than top-p.
Setting temperature to 0 is the simplest path to deterministic output.

code_example:
client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=msgs,
    temperature=0.7,
)

title: Top-k Sampling
short_definition: A sampling cutoff that keeps only the K most likely next tokens at each step, then samples among those — blocking the long tail of unlikely tokens entirely.
full_explanation:
After a forward pass, the model produces probabilities over the entire vocabulary (often 32K-200K tokens). Most of those tokens have near-zero probability — the long tail. Top-k truncates this: keep only the K highest-probability tokens, renormalize so they sum to 1, then sample from the truncated distribution. Common K values: 40-100 for general use, 5-10 for more conservative output, hundreds for more diverse output.

Top-k was the standard sampling strategy from the GPT-2 era through 2020. It's simple, fast, and prevents the worst long-tail garbage. The known weakness: K is fixed regardless of context. In high-confidence states (the top token has probability 0.9), K=40 is way too generous — it includes tokens with probability 0.001 alongside the dominant token. In low-confidence states, K=40 might be too restrictive.

Top-p (nucleus) sampling addressed this by switching to cumulative-probability cutoff. Min-p sampling addresses it differently with a relative-probability threshold. Top-k remains widely used, often combined with top-p (apply both filters; sample from the intersection). On most modern serving stacks it's exposed alongside top-p as an additional safety filter.
why_it_matters: Top-k is the easiest sampling concept to understand and a useful building block. Even when you've moved on to top-p or min-p as your primary cutoff, K=100 as a co-filter is harmless and prevents the tail-end pathologies. For learning sampling intuitions, start here — once you see how K trades off variety for safety, the other strategies make immediate sense as refinements. For production, it's increasingly a backup rather than primary cutoff.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine the model laying out all possible next words and you can only consider the top 40 — the rest get crossed off. Among those 40, you roll the weighted die. Top-k is 'I'll consider this many candidates and no more.' The number is fixed regardless of how confident the model is, which is its blessing (predictable behavior) and curse (sometimes too generous, sometimes too tight).
see_it_in_practice: HuggingFace: `model.generate(input_ids, do_sample=True, top_k=50, temperature=0.8)`. OpenAI doesn't expose top_k directly (only top_p). Anthropic exposes top_k as an option. Setting top_k=1 is equivalent to greedy decoding.

related_terms_explained.connection: Top-p replaces fixed-count cutoff with cumulative-probability cutoff — context-aware where top-k is fixed.
Min-p uses relative-probability cutoff — another evolution beyond top-k.
Top-k filters the candidates; temperature shapes the distribution among them.
