Top-k=1 reduces to greedy decoding.

code_example:
out = model.generate(
    input_ids,
    do_sample=True,
    top_k=50,
    temperature=0.8,
)

title: Top-p (Nucleus) Sampling
short_definition: A sampling cutoff that keeps the smallest set of tokens whose probabilities add up to at least P (e.g., 0.9), then samples from that set — adapting cutoff size to model confidence.
full_explanation:
Top-p sampling (Holtzman et al., 2019), also called nucleus sampling, fixes top-k's main flaw: a fixed token count is wrong because the model's confidence varies wildly. Top-p instead picks the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds P, then samples from that set.

When the model is confident (top token = 0.9), the cumulative-90% nucleus might be just 1-2 tokens — top-p naturally stays narrow. When the model is uncertain (top token = 0.05), the nucleus might expand to 50+ tokens — top-p naturally widens. The cutoff adapts to the situation in a way top-k cannot.

Top-p became the dominant sampling strategy for chat-style generation around 2020 and remains the default in most APIs (OpenAI exposes it, Anthropic exposes it, all open serving stacks expose it). Common values: 0.9-0.95 for chat, 0.8 for more focused output, 1.0 to disable. It's almost always combined with temperature; the typical advice is to tune one and leave the other near default.

Known weaknesses: in very high-uncertainty states, the 90% nucleus can include tokens that genuinely don't make sense — the long tail of plausible-but-wrong choices. Min-p sampling addresses this with a relative-probability floor instead. For most production use, top-p with values 0.9-0.95 plus temperature 0.7 is the safe default.
why_it_matters: If you're using an LLM API and don't have a strong reason to do otherwise, you're probably using top-p whether you realize it or not — it's the default in most chat APIs. Understanding what it actually does (adaptive cutoff at cumulative probability mass) explains a lot of model behavior: why output sometimes gets generic, why it sometimes drifts, why low-confidence prompts produce more varied responses. Foundational knowledge for anyone tuning generation in production.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine ranking all next-token candidates by probability and walking down the list, adding to your 'consider' bag until the bag's total weight is 90%. In a confident moment, the bag fills with just a couple of high-weight items. In an uncertain moment, the bag fills with many small-weight items. You then pick from the bag by weighted draw. The bag's size adapts to the situation — that adaptation is the whole point. Top-k's fixed bag size misses this.
see_it_in_practice: OpenAI: `top_p=0.9` is roughly default for chat. Test the same prompt at top_p=0.7 (more focused) and top_p=1.0 (full distribution) — you'll see the variance band. For creative work, pair top_p=0.95 with temperature 0.9. For factual extraction, pair top_p=0.9 with temperature 0.2.

related_terms_explained.connection: Top-p replaces top-k's fixed-count cutoff with adaptive cumulative-probability cutoff.
Min-p improves on top-p in low-confidence regimes by using relative-probability instead of cumulative.
Top-p truncates; temperature shapes the distribution within the truncated set.
Top-p is multinomial sampling with a specific filter applied to the distribution.

code_example:
client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=msgs,
    top_p=0.9,
    temperature=0.7,
)

title: Typical Sampling
short_definition: A sampling strategy that picks tokens whose surprise (information content) is closest to the average expected surprise, instead of just the most-likely tokens.
full_explanation:
Top-p and top-k both filter by raw probability — keep the most-likely candidates. Typical sampling (Meister et al., 2022) takes a different angle from information theory. The expected information content (entropy) of the distribution at each step gives you a target 'natural surprise' value. Typical sampling keeps only tokens whose individual information content is close to that target.

Mechanically: compute entropy H of the next-token distribution. For each token, compute its information content I_t = -log(P(t)). Keep tokens where |I_t - H| is small (within a threshold, parameter `typical_p`). Sample from those.

The theoretical motivation: human-written text has remarkably stable per-token information content. Most published writing sits in a narrow band of typicality. Probabilities in the very-high or very-low tails of the distribution often correspond to either generic boilerplate (high prob, low surprise) or hallucinated content (low prob, high surprise). Sampling near the typical band tends to produce more 'natural' prose.

In practice, results are mixed — some tasks improve, some don't. Llama.cpp and HuggingFace ship it as an option. It's most useful in creative writing where 'typical' prose character matters more than chasing the most-likely tokens.
why_it_matters: Niche but interesting. For most production work top-p remains better-tested and more predictable. For creative writing on local models — particularly when output feels mechanical even with min-p and temperature tuning — typical sampling is worth experimenting with. It's a different angle on the same problem (which tokens to keep at sampling time) and occasionally produces noticeably better prose. Mostly useful as part of a sampling-tuning toolkit, not as a default.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine measuring the average surprise level of every word in a paragraph of human writing — there's a typical band, and most words fall in it. Sentences with too many unusually predictable words feel boring; sentences with too many unusually surprising words feel jarring. Typical sampling tells the model: stay in the band that real writers stay in. Don't always pick the highest-probability boring word; don't pick the very-low-probability shocking word; pick something appropriately surprising.
see_it_in_practice: In HuggingFace: `model.generate(input_ids, do_sample=True, typical_p=0.9, temperature=1.0)`. Best tested on creative-writing prompts where the difference is most visible. Compare outputs across top-p, min-p, and typical at the same temperature — picking the right cutoff for your task is often a matter of taste.

related_terms_explained.connection: Both filter the distribution; top-p uses cumulative probability, typical uses information-content matching.
Typical sampling targets the natural information content; perplexity is the same quantity exponentiated.
Both are alternatives to top-p that handle low-confidence states differently.
Both use entropy as a control signal but apply it differently — typical filters tokens, entropy decoding picks strategy.

code_example:
out = model.generate(
    input_ids,
    do_sample=True,
    typical_p=0.9,
    temperature=1.0,
)
